speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three, an interview with Regina Mauskopf. You were describing the-- the situation in Mukacevo. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, as I say, they take from the ground, you know, for the making the brick. They were not doing that time. It was already like discarded, that whole thing. But it was still just staying. And from wherever they took the ground for the bricks to make, it was already like swamps, you know, kind of like things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we wash there our dish there. We washed our clothes there. It started to get-- they said that-- that people seen lice already. You know, from that kind of-- you know, it's-- but what can you do? They just couldn't do nothing else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- as I say, in-- in-- in-- like I say, all right, we had here a family from [INAUDIBLE], or something. But there was a long things. As I say, that-- you're losing that, your main thing that-- Well, you got dressed. You had to get dressed and everybody's there. There is the-- the-- the family from my-- my girlfriend. And there's another family. And there is, like, you know. It was really tough.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we'd been there for-- for almost like-- well, took us away like in April 21 when it started, after Pesach, on a Friday. So we'd been there like about three weeks. I don't know. Because the 15th of-- of May, we arrived in Auschwitz. In that-- in that time did they ever tell you where you were going to go? How long you were going to stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Somebody said that they said that they're gonna-- how should I say-- that they're going to put us in a different place for work. Things like that. But that's all. No. And nobody-- kind of everybody was kind of pulled in, in itself. Well, people were talking to each other. Do you know, do you know, do you know? But-- but there was nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we've been there for the-- the things. And-- and as I say, we saved the stuff, the-- the cookies, the everything, whatever it is, whatever was left. We were afraid to eat enough, you know, and remain there. Because they came like a Sunday morning. It was that everybody should just whatever he has on himself, the clothes, and nothing else he could take anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we couldn't take a lot-- a lot of stuff from home. But from home too we put on two, three dresses, except whatever you put in the suitcases. Whatever. And-- and pajamas underneath and-- and whatever we could stash on ourselves. And-- and then we-- and they said that over there in this and this place, everybody should go there out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you go on, was there anyone there during that period of time that were sick? I mean, did they-- did they have facilities for people that were sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't even know what it is. I know one thing that the next-- like-- he was my girlfriend's family and then that other little piece was that [? Jusevitch ?] family. And he got a little stroke or whatever. Because in the morning, when he got up one morning, his mouth was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Crooked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Crooked on one side, and he could-- it affected his speech. But I don't remember it there. Something was done, nothing. Did anyone try to leave or escape during that time? Not really. Not really. Because maybe if somebody, we don't know really what, where, or what. Maybe some did, I don't know about it. I don't know about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any common prayer, davening, tefillin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Everybody had the [INAUDIBLE], probably the tallits too. I-- I know about the [INAUDIBLE] because when the-- the-- we were kind of-- that Sunday morning, when we start to leave everything, and-- well, as I say, my father, we had our brother over there. You know. Kind of-- he was 42, 45, 46, I don't know. Well, he was more. What am I talking? Yeah, something like that because he was born in 1856, and that was '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what he has his own family kind of to-- to worry about what to take and what not to take. So my father said to me like this. Look, Regina, I'm putting here-- it was kind of like those beams between that kind of thing. You know, [? and the kind ?] to push underneath. He says, I'm putting the [INAUDIBLE] over there. I don't know who's gonna come back. There it is. That's where it is. That was-- oh, yeah. There was that one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, naturally we had to watch out for-- if the police came. It was not-- you know, kind of-- they will come in our things and always with that stick. And-- and I remember my father was-- my mother had kind of like for a [NON-ENGLISH], kind of like a big shawl, fringes. And he was feeling cold so we gave him that shawl and throwed it around, the fringes were hanging. You know, kind-- shawl, there's kind of like a shawl around the neck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when they were coming in, the police and gendarmes, you know, everybody had to start-- stand up in attention. And he took his stick and kind of picked up, kind of like this, that shawl, and said who is that, the king? Because he was kind of like [? Norman. ?] You know, he was straight-- straight up thing. And he had the beard was already [? done. ?] And that shawl there hanging, that thing, said who is that, the king? In Hungarian.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- it was-- so then, when that Sunday, when it came already, we went out. And my mother said she's cold. Well, we can't take nothing. We have to leave the blanket. We have to leave whatever we had, kind of, things. We had our bed spread took from home. It was green with velvet. It was a warm thing. It was a very warm thing. Instead of blanket we took it, kind of something else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she rolled it up like soldiers, you know, on the front, rolled it up and putting it kind of like-- like across and tied it somehow. I don't remember with what, how-- but it was across. And they looked everybody over, what we have, you know, if we don't have. Sometimes they even kind of did with the stick by the pocket if they're not gonna feel something, you know, in the pocket. And when it came to my mother, and again, they said-- did the same thing with that stick. What's that? And mother said, I'm an old woman, and I'm maybe gonna be cold. And then he started beating her with it over the head. And you know, she should take it off. How could you take it off if you have to grab your head or your hand, you know, where he was hitting? And here we were staying kind of family, grown up people with other fists like this, clenched together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Until my brother kind of made like a step froward, kind of like he wanted like to go, but somebody, I mean, I don't even know that person who was standing the same thing, you know, for the same, and grabbed him in the back and said to him, you're going to make more trouble. He and me start kind of like gesturing for my mother take it off. So until she didn't took that off, they were hitting her with the stick. So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- well-- [INAUDIBLE]. That-- then we went into the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she take it off, by the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. She took it somehow. She took it. And she was struggling. And, you know? And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- Anyway, after that they put us in the cattle car.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was the car, the train, from-- from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not far away. It was not far away. Because, you see, it was the tracks by the factory, the-- the thing. You know. The-- it was kind of-- you know, like-- not-- it was-- it's a freight, you know, cattle car, freight car. So they were probably transferring bricks from there, or whatever. I don't know what they-- you know, but it was not far away. Just a walk, maybe a half an hour, maybe even less. I don't even remember how originally we went to the train there. And in the train--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it morning, noon, night?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was night. It was already late in the afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: That-- that you walked?

speaker: interviewee
text: That Sunday. Yeah. That-- because we slept there. We-- I think-- this I don't know exactly. If we started-- I know we were there in the train and sleeping. And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Yeah. We slept there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In the train. We slept there in the train because the next day, we went towards Kashau. And in Kashau, that's in Slovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: But do you remember being in the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. And there were other people too, not just-- not just ready, some from far away villages. Also, was that we're really far in the Carpathian, towards another direction, towards Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was a cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: A cattle car.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you able to sit or did you have to stand?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was not crowded.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was not crowded.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not crowded. It was really not crowded because I remember kind of walking around there. And there was dirty straw, things like that. And we had enough place to sleep. There's-- there were a sister and a brother with a mother. You were this far away from each other, you know, because we-- the thing. So we were kind of not really crowded in that train.

speaker: interviewer
text: For about how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think in Kashau, they-- they put a little bit more people. Not much, but a little bit more because in the-- we asked for water till we get-- got to Kashau. You know, through the window we asked the thing. Or even in Kashau when we stopped. We start asking for water when the train stopped. And the Hungarians, they don't want to give us.

speaker: interviewer
text: No food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No water. But then the Germans took us over. The-- the SS, or the Wehrmacht, I don't remember who told. But the Germans took us over. They gave water, whoever asked. They gave water. And, you know, we asked them between ourselves, how come they give us water and they didn't? Maybe, you know, it's like this sometimes. You get an order don't do it, and they're afraid for them. They would maybe not be as, you know, they were afraid for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any place where you could relieve yourself in a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh that-- that's another humiliation. [INAUDIBLE]. It was a bucket in the thing. And-- it's-- you know, somebody had to go from our family, so the rest of the family stood kind of like-- like you know, with our backs to-- to the person. And that's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you tolerate the smell?

speaker: interviewee
text: It has to be. It had to be. And when we were going already towards the Polish, you know, we went through the Tatras. You know the Tatras?

speaker: interviewer
text: The mountains.

speaker: interviewee
text: The mountains. And, as I said, I-- I was always kind of like being on-- on the safe side. Certain things if I could do, I had a spoon with me. And my sister [INAUDIBLE] she had one, a little small baby. And we went to the window, you know, wherever I wanted to go because we had enough place to the window for fresh air. And it was kind of like drizzling outside. You know, first we put out the hand and lick the-- the fingers. And then I took the spoon, whatever came into the spoon. So things were like that. Till we-- and we were going and going and going.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how long? How many--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, I want to tell you something. Now, as a Jewish girl should not have done that with-- with-- with a chunk of bacon. I mean, like Canadian bacon. It was smoked. The neighbor [INAUDIBLE], which I-- we gave them to hide things, she told me that night, she said, Regina-- and-- well, in-- in Russian they said instead [INAUDIBLE], they said [INAUDIBLE], said, I want to give you something.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said well, what do you know? She said I know, but sometimes when-- when you are in trouble if you want, you want. So I took that all the time. Till Mukachevo, I didn't talk for nobody. But in Mukachevo, I told them the hunger start already, a little bit kind of present. So I told from my [INAUDIBLE]. I said, do you know what I have? She gave me [INAUDIBLE], that's her husband's name was Theodore Ferken, they called him. And I told her that I have a thing in my suitcase. A piece of bacon.

speaker: interviewee
text: A piece of bacon. It was not getting bad because that could have stayed. It was smoked out. Kind of like that. It could stay without refrigeration. And I said should we tell for my parents. You know. She said, no, till we have our-- whatever we have, let's see when it-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- how do I say?

speaker: interviewer
text: A greater need.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know. And so when we were going already in the-- in the cattle car towards Auschwitz without food, then I said to her again, to my sister, I said what do we do with it? I put that in my bosom because you see, there was nothing into the hem the-- the-- the thing, so I had it in my bosom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to her, let's go and tell our parents. You know, and they were sitting there in the corner, mother and father, and-- sitting. And-- so we both went over and said, listen, I know I've done something, which I should not have done, but so and so, she gave me, the neighbor, that stink. Shall we cut? And let's give for whoever wants, you know, piece.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said that, my father said that to us, listen, we are old. Maybe you're gonna need it more. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and that was it. Well, it went-- the-- I took it-- I didn't-- you know, with me and it-- it wound up in delousing in Auschwitz with the clothes, with the hair, with everything there on the floor. That's what happened to that piece of bacon.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never ate it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Never gave it to anyone else to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how long were you on the train? Approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the next day, like the afternoon. If you came to that, you saw it on the television, that big gate there where they saw. And the train stopped. And while as I say-- said, we been there by that little window. I mean, whoever wanted to go, we-- we let them go. But everybody wanted to take a look. Everybody, you know, when the train stopped everybody, you know, they let ourselves look outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Train stopped. Where are we? Oh, well, wait a minute. As before, we passed Krakow. In Krakow stopped this car, the train. Till Krakow it didn't. Don't remember should have stopped. And as we stop think, and they were Polish people working on the-- on the tracks. And somebody said, does anybody know Polish? So my father, he knew a little bit Polish. Well, the Russian and Polish is a Slavic language anyway. So my father went to the little window there and asked the guy, he was there with that pick. What it's called. And says, could you tell us where we are going? Because we saw on the thing it's Krakow, on-- on the station.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he then-- and he showed like this. My father slumped down there, right, you know, behind the car on the floor. And he didn't talk for-- for for hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you also see this? Or he just told you? Or how did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I'd been there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw it. Yeah. Yeah. I was always kind of hanging. And I was like-- I was at home like a tomboy, kind of worked with my father in the fields a lot, kind of real work, like with those big sickles and-- and the thing. You cut the-- the-- the rye. You know. And-- so, yes. Yes. There by the window and he slumped down. And I didn't took much from Krakow to get to Auschwitz. Because it was just another hour maybe, or something like that. And then when we came to Auschwitz, and when the train stopped, and-- and my mother went to the window. And I was standing there with her too. And she-- and she said-- we saw a-- a scene there. There was a wagon with two horses and they were sitting two German soldiers. And there was a boy, I don't know, he was eight, maybe 10, maybe 11, I don't know. Or maybe 12. But small. And he had on that stripey clothes on him. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know, don't remember. It was kind of like they had like a little whip, a little stick but a small whip. You know. And the-- and whipped him. But he was just doing, kind of giving like a-- a sign for the boy. And the boy was jumping down from the-- from the-- went down and back up. And he was laughing, the little boy. You know. And-- and my mother said, look, look, look. Children. She said look at this, they're playing. You know. Well, they did things like that for propaganda. Probably that's what afterwards we found out that that's what they did. At that time, we didn't know. We were just happy that-- you know, my mother was happy that children are there. And look how he's laughing, and they're playing with him, the Germans. So we stood there for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you arrive in the daytime or night?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the daytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Daytime.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think it was late in the afternoon. It was daytime because we saw the whole thing. The [INAUDIBLE] were open. And there came those-- the Jewish boys. And they kept on saying like that the Germans shouldn't hear. You know, like with the thing, he said, whoever is developed, like could be 14 or 13, and if you're developed, like look like a 16 or a 17, go to the right, go to the right. And that's how he was kind of like jumping around between us, you know. So, you know, I don't know. A lot of-- maybe he went further, they did the same thing. But I know that's what it was by the front of our opening train. And then when I heard that-- and-- but it was going so fast, everything. As we went out, and I see like those wires. The-- the-- there was the gate that way, and here was a long kind of path to go back that way. That was the left side. And here was kind of the right side. And as-- by the time, you know, we kind of got-- you know, got-- we went out, and I see my parents, my sister is the one holds the baby. You know, the-- the sister on the hand and the other one there, and the mother of the two nieces, which were still kind of like-- which she writes in-- in the letter for me afterwards that I didn't let her go with her mother. That's how she thanked me that she's alive. And she start hollering out their names. She said, come on, you gonna get lost. Come on. I just saw my-- I didn't have time for my parents to say mother or father, or whatever, because she said about the girls. And I just said to her, don't worry. I'll take care on her. And she was already farther than the wall. Just holler to her. And that's all what I saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and my father from the back. And there the brother, and his wife with the two children, and my sister, and the other sister, and the sister-in-law. That was that. And then when we grabbed ourself and there was my cousin's daughter. I had two daughters because my-- the 14-year-old was a daughter too. And I-- and we kind of got-- you know, you get kind of like a lost for a moment. The parents are going, you know, and you stay here alone. You stay, you didn't stay, I don't know how long. It was seconds. You were pushed. Kind of, you were going. It was not far away, kind of to that gate from the train. And there was Mengele by the things. Well, naturally, we saw some people even which were going that way, some people-- young people were limping. You know, or they were cripples. You know--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're describing Dr. Mengele?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, [? hear ?] on television you could see his teeth. You know, that kind-- that grin. But I remember that-- that here, somehow by the eyes, something with the nose, but the grin, I didn't see.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in your mind, it was Mengele?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know that that's Mengele. But when they start telling us inside, you know, whatever. So we went in there-- we then went in those barracks when we went the second time. And you see, you know, in a row. We went in those houses, like Birkenau, or whatever. We didn't know what it was. But there were those [INAUDIBLE] inside, but it was kind of buildings.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did Mengele do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he just kind of-- we had to like turn around.

speaker: interviewer
text: He examined you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really examination. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he looked at you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just looked, yeah. In case somebody was limping or he was back into that, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were-- you were in the-- in a row going to the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't know whether it made a difference.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know that that's a right side or a left side that time. We just didn't know. We just didn't know. When we came in into their barracks and there was already the Polish people, girls, were already there from way before. So they knew the whole situation. We started to ask right away, where are our parents, the rest of the family? And one said-- well, that's later, about the zahlappell. And one said, whoever comes in here through the gate, it goes out through the chimney. But we didn't know what the chimney means. You know. That's what she said. Later on, when we were going for zahlappell outside, and it was kind of cold. It was, you know, in-- in May and we were worried about the parents. They haven't got clothes, they're cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we asked them again. I said, well, what do the parents are doing? Oh, she said, they are build chairs from cement and zahlappell. They have zahlappell too, but they sit in chairs. You know, they were kind of talking. That's what it was. So we been there and it was-- kind of-- you were thirsty. Well, first we went into the delousing shower. And then we came there. And then we got somehow, we didn't got those stripes things, clothes. But you remember taking a shower?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember whether it was cold or warm or hot?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was kind of like not really cold. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had to-- you had to disrobe completely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well we went inside and-- and, as I said, disrobed completely. And you sit on, there was kind of like benches. And we sit down and the-- the boys wash hair, Jewish boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And boys, men, they were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men. Men. Men were attending to us. Yeah. Men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though you were undressed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they were [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end up tape number 3.
